payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
C2PA (the Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity) is good work. The standard embeds signed metadata into media files, and the news and photo industries are starting to adopt it seriously. If you've seen a Content Credentials badge on an Associated Press wire photo or in Adobe products, that's C2PA.

It's also not going to solve music's provenance problem.

That isn't a knock on the standard. It's a knock on the assumption that one provenance system fits all media. C2PA was designed around a workflow that doesn't match how music actually moves through the industry.

The pipeline mismatch

C2PA assumes a short chain of custody: a creator captures or makes something, a publisher signs and distributes it, a consumer can verify the chain in a viewer that respects the credentials. Three parties, two transitions, all aligned on preserving the metadata.

Music has five or more parties between the artist and the listener: producer, distributor, streaming platform, mechanical rights organization (the body that collects royalties for the underlying composition when a song is streamed), and performance rights organization (the body that collects royalties when a song is played in public, on the radio, or in a venue). Each one re-encodes, repackages, or strips metadata as a matter of course. WAV files become MP3s. MP3s get transcoded again at the streaming platform. ID3 tags (the metadata fields embedded inside an MP3 file) get rewritten. By the time a song reaches a listener's earbuds, almost nothing of its origin metadata is intact.

A standard that assumes metadata persists won't survive that pipeline. The mechanics of music distribution are actively hostile to it.mance rights organization. Each one re-encodes, repackages, or strips metadata as a matter of course. WAV becomes MP3. MP3 gets transcoded again at the streaming platform. ID3 tags get rewritten. By the time a song reaches a listener's earbuds, almost nothing of its origin metadata is intact.

A standard that assumes metadata persists won't survive that pipeline. The mechanics of music distribution are actively hostile to it.

C2PA pipeline versus music distribution pipeline
Two pipelines compared. C2PA's three-step pipeline preserves metadata end to end. Music's six-step pipeline strips metadata at each transition.

C2PA ASSUMES

Creator

Publisher

Viewer

credentials preserved end to end

MUSIC ACTUALLY DOES

Artist

Producer

Distributor

DSP

CMO

Listener

credentials degrade at each handoff

The authorship mismatch

C2PA signs "who made this." Music has at least four answers to that question for any given song: the songwriter, the performer, the producer, and the master rights holder (the entity, usually a label, that owns the recording itself). These aren't always the same person, and the splits between them are the entire economic substrate of the recorded music industry.

A provenance system for music has to model split authorship as a first-class concept. It can't be bolted on after the fact.

The verification mismatch

C2PA's consumer story is built around viewers (photo software, browsers) that surface the credentials. Music consumers stream through apps owned by Spotify, Apple, Amazon, and YouTube. None of those apps have an obvious incentive to expose provenance UI to the user, and none of them are committing to.

A working music provenance system has to verify upstream, in the rights and royalty pipeline, before a song ever reaches a consumer surface. Listener-facing verification is a nice-to-have. The B2B verification is the whole point.

What music actually needs

Provenance for music has to do three things C2PA wasn't built for.

First, survive aggressive re-encoding and metadata stripping. The signature has to be robust to lossy transformation, not stored in a header that gets nuked at every transcode.

Second, encode multi-party authorship from the start. Songwriter, performer, producer, master rights holder, each with their own signed claim, the splits between them legible to the system.

Third, plug into the rights and royalty layer, not just the file layer. Provenance that doesn't talk to the rights organizations and the collection societies is a museum piece.

C2PA is a useful primitive. It's not a finished system for music. The work is building what music actually needs, which is a heavier lift than retrofitting a photo standard onto a fundamentally different pipeline.
payload.title: Why C2PA isn't enough for music

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
I founded my recording studio in Buenos Aires in 2016. I priced in US dollars from day one.

That sentence is more controversial in Argentina than it sounds anywhere else. The local economy operates in pesos. The peso has lost a meaningful share of its purchasing power in every year I've been in business. Pricing in dollars in that environment is read as either elitist, opportunistic, or both, depending on who's evaluating.

It was also, from the very first day, the only viable choice.

Why dollars, in plain terms

Buying the initial gear meant working within the cepo cambiario (in Argentina, cepo cambiario refers to the strict capital and currency controls the government imposes to limit access to US dollars; depending on the regime, individuals and businesses are restricted to small monthly dollar purchases at official rates, while a parallel "blue dollar" market operates at much higher prices). Professional studio gear is priced in dollars, sold by international vendors, and shipped through customs that wants to be paid in dollars. The math of getting a single piece of equipment into the country forced me to think in dollars before I'd booked my first session.

If the inputs are dollar-priced and the international clients who pay the bills are dollar-paying, pricing the work in pesos is just a slow-motion margin loss. Every project quoted today and delivered eight weeks from now becomes a different project economically by the time it ships. Pricing in dollars takes the variable out. The number quoted is the number that matters when the invoice clears.

Early on I didn't publicize it. The conversations were dollar-denominated but the public posture was peso-friendly. When I moved to the United States in 2021, the public posture changed too. Same pricing model, different surface.

What it actually taught me

The currency decision was the easy part. Circumstances forced it.

The discipline behind it was harder. Holding a number when the world around you is pressuring you to move it is a skill that doesn't come naturally to most people running creative businesses. You feel the pull of every conversation: the artist who's between labels, the producer with a tight project budget, the client who wants to negotiate as a relationship gesture rather than a financial one. Each of those is a legitimate ask. Each of them is also a chance to set a ceiling on every project that comes after.

The version of me that survived multiple Argentine currency cycles wasn't the version with the best gear or the loudest reputation. It was the version that understood the difference between negotiating scope, payment terms, and timeline (all flexible) and negotiating the headline number (rarely flexible).

Most of the freelance and studio pricing advice I see online treats the number as the thing to optimize. It isn't. The discipline of holding the number is the thing to optimize. The number is just the surface.

What I'd tell a younger version of myself

The number you quote is a statement about how you value your own time. The minute you treat it as negotiable, you've told the client your time is also negotiable. There's a way to handle scope, payment terms, and timeline flexibly without negotiating the headline number, and most freelancers haven't learned that distinction.

Pricing in dollars from Argentina is an extreme version of a problem every freelancer has, in any currency, in any market: the discipline of holding a number when there's pressure to move it. The pressure changes shape. The discipline is the same.

Nine years in, the math still works. So does the discipline.
payload.title: Pricing in dollars from Argentina

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
The previous note made the case that the complete authenticity signal in music distribution is the link between two stamps. One that confirms the artist is who they say they are. Another that confirms the track is what it claims to be. Linked at the moment of upload, the two together produce something a single stamp cannot: a verifiable claim that this human made this work.

The natural follow-up is operational. Where does the artist actually stamp the work?

Not at the studio

Asking artists to manage stamping at the studio is a UX failure waiting to happen. Production tools change project to project. Sessions involve multiple collaborators with different setups. The artist's stamp should not be entangled with whichever studio software they happened to open that afternoon.

The studio software stamps its own output. AI tools stamp theirs. Sample libraries stamp their contributions. Each of those describes what the tool did, not who the artist is.

The artist's stamp is different. It is a statement of authorship, not production. It belongs at the moment the artist actually publishes the work into the world.

Distribution is the natural place

Distributors are the right home for it. CD Baby, DistroKid, TuneCore, Believe, EMPIRE, and the rest of the indie distribution layer already verify artists at signup, hold their tax documents, manage their payout accounts, and require them to attest to authorship at upload. Adding a stamp to that existing attestation is a small addition on top of relationships that already exist.

The flow becomes clear. The work picks up stamps during production. By the time the file is ready for distribution, it carries a record of everything that touched it. The artist logs into their distributor, reviews what is attached to the file, and stamps it themselves. That stamp is the binding. It says: this work, with this history, is mine to release.

Streaming platforms verify the chain when they receive the file. Profile verification plus work verification plus the artist's binding stamp equals a complete authenticity signal at the point of ingest.

This also reframes the business case for distributors. They currently compete on reach, payout speed, and creator tools. Verified distribution becomes the next axis of competition. Sign your catalog with us is a real differentiator, especially for independent artists worried about voice cloning, scraped catalogs, or impersonation through fraudulent uploads.

Two ways to get a stamp

Artists should have two paths to a stamp, and the architecture should treat both as valid.

The first is the managed version. The distributor handles the stamp on the artist's behalf as part of onboarding. The artist logs in and clicks publish. The stamp is applied invisibly. This is how most other industries handle digital signing already. The user authenticates with the platform. The platform handles the rest. For working musicians who want to release music, not study cryptography, this is the right path.

The second is the independent version. The artist generates and holds their own stamp through a public registry, the same way anyone can register a domain name or a copyright. They can take that stamp with them across distributors, sign their catalog independently, and verify it against the registry from anywhere. This is the right path for established artists with large catalogs, for estates and indie labels managing legacy works, and for anyone who wants their authorship verifiable independent of any single platform.

Both paths produce the same result downstream: a verifiable artist stamp bound to a verifiable work stamp. The chain does not care which path the artist took. It only cares that the stamp checks out.

How adoption actually happens

This creates a graceful path forward. Distributors can start with the managed version because the cost to the artist is near zero. The independent version develops in parallel through standards work and public registries. Artists who want portability migrate when the tooling matures. Artists who never want to think about it never have to.

The verification is the same in both cases. The difference is who holds the stamp.
payload.title: Where artist signatures live

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
Most people who hear "music provenance" assume the goal is to catch AI-generated tracks. That's the wrong frame, and the framing is going to start mattering as the industry drafts policy around it.

A provenance system designed to catch AI inherits every problem detection has. It has to define what counts as AI, which becomes a moving target as the tools evolve. It has to gatekeep, which means false positives become a feature instead of a bug. And it has to keep up with whatever generators ship next, which is the same arms race the industry is already losing.

A provenance system designed to credit humans doesn't have any of those problems. It doesn't need to know what AI is doing on the other side. It just needs to verify that a specific human authored a specific work at a specific moment, and carry that proof through the rights pipeline.

The difference shows up immediately in the design choices.

What "anti-AI" framing leads to

Build a system to catch AI and you end up building gatekeeping infrastructure. You decide what does and doesn't qualify as "human enough." You build flagging mechanisms. You build appeals processes. You build a regulatory body to administer the boundary, because the boundary keeps moving.

You also build a system that fights its own users. An artist who uses a vocal correction plugin shouldn't have to defend their humanity. An artist who used a generative tool for one element of a track shouldn't have to negotiate a percentage with a verification authority. The minute the system has to make these calls, it's broken.

And the policy crowd will start drafting legislation around the wrong question. "How do we detect AI" becomes the legal frame, and the industry signs up for arms-race compliance forever.

What "pro-human" framing leads to

Build a system to credit humans and the design narrows. You only need to verify three things at the moment of creation: who, what, and when. The signature attaches at that point and travels with the work. Whether AI was involved upstream or downstream is somebody else's question. The system stays neutral and stays simple.

This also changes who the system is for. Anti-AI provenance is for platforms and regulators trying to filter what reaches consumers. Pro-human provenance is for artists trying to get paid for what they made. The first one creates new gatekeepers. The second one strengthens the existing infrastructure that already pays artists, just with cleaner signal.

The pro-human frame also handles the messy reality of how music actually gets made. Co-writers, session players, producers, samplers, engineers. All of them sign their contributions. The system doesn't have to know which contribution was made with what tool. It only has to know who claims it and prove the claim is verifiable.

Why this matters now

The framing is being set right now, in policy drafts, in standards meetings, in the trade press. Most of it is being set by the anti-AI frame, because that's what's politically loud.

If the industry locks in "provenance equals AI detection" as the default mental model, the working systems built later will inherit that framing whether they want to or not. Regulatory hearings will use it. DSP terms of service will use it. Royalty collection societies will write it into their procedures.

Get the frame right while it's still up for grabs. The technology should serve the humans who made the work, not the institutions trying to police what counts as work in the first place.
payload.title: Provenance is for humans, not against AI

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
I've integrated AI-assisted tools into my production workflow across more than 150 commercial releases since 2022. Some of those tools save real time. Others are improving fast. A few are still mostly hype.

The line between the three isn't where most coverage puts it. The honest version, from someone whose deadlines depend on getting this right.

What actually saves time

Source separation and cleanup. Stem isolation (pulling the individual instruments and vocals out of a finished mix), vocal extraction, hum and noise removal. The current generation of these tools is genuinely excellent and replaces work that used to take hours of manual editing. This is the category where I save the most time per project.

Transcription and session documentation. Auto-transcription for interview-style sessions, voice memos from artists, reference notes during writing camps. Saves administrative time that otherwise comes out of creative time.

Specific repair tasks. De-noising, click and pop removal, certain kinds of phase correction. The work was always doable manually. The current tools just do it faster and often more cleanly.

Reference matching for mix and master decisions. Using AI-assisted analysis to compare a mix against a reference track on specific spectral and dynamic dimensions (the frequency balance and the loudness behavior). Doesn't make the decision for me. Tells me where the gap is faster than my ears alone.

That's the high-confidence list. Reliable, daily, time-saving.

What's improving fast

Mixing assistants. A few years ago these produced a defensible starting point and not much more. The current generation is meaningfully better, particularly for genre-specific starting templates, balance suggestions on dense arrangements, and as a sanity check before a final pass. I still steer them toward the vision rather than letting them drive, but the steering takes less time than it used to. For a junior engineer they're a real accelerant. For experienced engineers they're a useful second opinion.

Mastering tools with adaptive processing. The label "AI mastering" has been applied to a wide range of tools, some doing genuinely adaptive analysis and some just rebadging older signal processing. The adaptive ones have improved noticeably and earn their place in the chain for certain project types, particularly fast-turnaround and reference-based work. I wouldn't hand a flagship release to one without close oversight, but the gap between assistive mastering and manual mastering is narrowing every release cycle.

These aren't slam-dunk time-savers yet. They're getting closer.

What isn't there yet

Generative tools for full production. Generating a track or a stem from a prompt sounds like a time-saver until you try to integrate the output into a session. The generated material rarely matches the key, tempo, or sonic identity of the project closely enough to be usable without significant rework. By the time you've reworked it, you'd have been faster recording or programming the part directly.

AI feedback on artistic choices. This one's more about framing than capability. The model is averaging across whatever it was trained on, and averaging isn't useful as a creative note. The artists I work with want sharp opinions, not averages. That doesn't mean the tools are bad. It means they're being asked to do the wrong job.

The pattern underneath

The tools that save time are the ones doing well-defined deterministic tasks. The tools that are improving fastest are the ones doing genre-aware assistive work where a human steers. The tools that aren't there yet are the ones being asked to make purely creative or aesthetic decisions in isolation.

That's not an argument against generative AI in music. It's a snapshot of where the technology actually is from inside the workflow, today, on commercial deadlines. The categories will keep moving. The honest read is that AI has changed how I work in real ways, mostly on the boring parts of production, with a growing footprint on the assistive layer above.

The creative core is still mine. That's not a complaint.
payload.title: Where AI actually saves time in record production

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
Spotify announced "Verified by Spotify" the week of June 17, 2026. The framing was clear. In 2026, with AI-generated music at scale, authenticity means something different. The product confirms that an artist profile belongs to the person it claims to belong to.

That solves a real problem. Impersonation has been a quiet crisis for years. Fake accounts under real artists' names. Fake albums uploaded to verified profiles. Fake catalogs built to harvest playlist placements. Spotify confirming the human behind the profile is a meaningful first move.

But it is a first move, not a complete one.

Two questions, not one

Authenticity in music distribution actually asks two questions, and they are not the same.

The first question is about the artist. Who is this account? Is the person behind it the artist they claim to be? Verified by Spotify answers this one.

The second question is about the track. What is this song? Who made it? Was it generated, sampled, scraped, or composed by the artist whose name is on it? Verifying the profile says nothing about what gets uploaded to the profile.

A verified artist can still upload tracks of unverified origin. The 85% of mass AI uploads Deezer flagged earlier this year as linked to fraud or artificial streaming sit in this second question, not the first. So do the impersonation cases where someone uploads a fake album under another artist's name through a third-party distributor. Profile verification does not reach into the file.

The binding

The complete answer is not either question alone. It is the link between them.

Imagine two stamps on every release. One stamp says, this is really the artist. The other stamp says, this is really their work. When the two stamps are linked together at the moment of upload, you have something that cannot be faked: a verifiable claim that this specific human made this specific track. Either the stamps check out or they don't. There is no probabilistic middle. There is no third state where a detection model gives a confidence score that gets disputed in a royalty dispute.

This is the structural difference between detection and signed authorship. Detection identifies probabilistically. It can be argued. Signed authorship proves directly. It cannot. The binding is what turns two separate stamps into a single, unfalsifiable claim.

How real music actually works

The model accommodates the way music actually gets made.

Features and collaborations carry multiple artist stamps on one work. Each one verifies independently. The track carries proof of every contributor.

Ghostwriting and co-production work the same way, with stamps that can be public or private depending on what the parties agreed to. The chain stays intact regardless of which credits surface publicly.

Sample libraries and AI tools stamp their own contributions when they are used. The artist's stamp on the final work either includes those upstream stamps or doesn't. Either way, the source of every part of the track is legible.

Disputed authorship becomes a question of which stamps verify, not whose detection model is more accurate. Direct proof beats probability every time.

What's still missing

Spotify shipped the first stamp the week of June 29, 2026. That is the easier of the two problems because verifying who someone is has decades of precedent across every other industry that uses the internet.

Verifying what they made is harder. It requires every tool in the production chain to stamp its output. It requires a place where the artist's stamp gets bound to the work's stamp. The math behind all of this is well understood and used everywhere from banking to passports. Music just hasn't built its version yet.

The broader argument is in Provenance Over Detection, published earlier this year. This note adds the specific mechanism the Spotify announcement made worth naming. The binding is what completes the trust stack.
payload.title: Verifying the artist isn't enough

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
A meaningful share of the global music royalty pool ends up in the wrong place. The exact number depends on which collection society you ask, which territory, which year. Conservative industry estimates put it in the hundreds of millions annually in the United States alone, with billions sitting in unallocated pools globally.

This pool has a name: the black box. Money that was collected from someone who used a song, but couldn't be paid out to the rights holder because the system couldn't figure out who the rights holder was.

The reflexive explanation is that the music rights system is corrupt or broken. That isn't quite right. The system isn't broken. The metadata is.

What actually happens

When a song gets used (streamed, broadcast, performed, sampled), a payment gets generated. To route that payment to the right person, the collection society has to match the use to a song, the song to a writer, the writer to a publisher, and the publisher to a payee. Every step depends on metadata.

If the metadata is clean, the match happens automatically and the money flows. If the metadata is messy, the match fails, and the money sits in suspense until someone manually reconciles it. If the manual reconciliation also fails, the money stays in the black box. After a few years, depending on the territory, it gets redistributed to the catalog at large or to other rights holders entirely.

Most industry effort goes into cleaning up the metadata downstream. Reconciliation tools, ISWC matching services, AI-powered title-matching algorithms. These help, marginally. They don't solve the problem because they're working downstream of where the problem actually starts.

Where collected music royalties end up
A horizontal bar divided into four segments showing the distribution of collected music royalties: matched and paid, matched late, black box, and misallocated. The first segment is the largest, but a visible leakage area covers the remaining three categories. Proportions are illustrative.

WHERE COLLECTED ROYALTIES END UP

leakage

Matched and paid

Matched late

Black box

Misallocated

Illustrative. Proportions vary by territory, year, and collection society.

Why downstream cleanup can't catch up

The pipeline that generates the metadata problem is the same one I described in the C2PA piece. Every handoff between the artist, the producer, the distributor, the DSP, and the collection society is an opportunity for metadata to drop, get rewritten, or get associated with the wrong identifier.

By the time a song is generating royalties, its metadata has been touched by five or more parties, each of which had different assumptions about format, identifier system, and which fields matter. Reconciling that downstream is a forensic exercise.

Provenance doesn't try to reconstruct the chain after the fact. It signs the chain at creation and carries it through. That's the structural difference. Metadata cleanup is archaeology. Provenance is title insurance.

What changes with provenance

A provenance system doesn't eliminate black box money on day one. It eliminates the cause that creates new black box money on day two and beyond. Each work that enters the pipeline with verified authorship arrives at the collection society with an unbroken chain back to the human who made it. The match doesn't require reconciliation because the match was established at creation.

Over time, the black box stops growing for new works. Existing catalog still requires the cleanup tools and the manual reconciliation. The economic case for the system is the difference between those two curves.

This is why provenance is a label and rights holder problem first, and an artist problem second. The black box is real money sitting somewhere it doesn't belong, and the longer the industry treats it as an inevitable cost of doing business, the more of that money walks out the door every year.
payload.title: Music's billion-dollar metadata problem

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
When I moved to the United States in 2021, I started running a freelance practice in parallel with my Buenos Aires studio. Five years later, that practice spans clients in the US and Argentina, working entirely remotely.

The infrastructure to make that work is unglamorous. The discipline behind it is the actual lesson.

Most "remote work" content focuses on tools. The tools are the easy part. The hard part is scoping, and scoping is what nobody writes about.

What I learned about scope

A remote project across timezones is a forecasting exercise. Every "let me get back to you tomorrow" is actually 24 to 36 hours of dead time depending on which way the timezones run. Three of those in a project, and you've added a week to delivery without doing any extra work.

Scope on a remote project has to be tighter than scope on a local one. Not by a little. By a lot. The scope discipline that lets a local studio survive a few "while you're at it" requests will quietly destroy a remote project's timeline because each request triggers a multi-day round-trip.

The fix isn't saying no more often. It's structuring scope so the round-trips happen on questions that actually matter, not on revisions that should have been part of the original brief.

What I learned about cultural variance

US clients tend to scope tight upfront and then expect the spec to hold. If something needs to change, it's a formal change request. That works well remotely.

Latin American clients tend to scope looser upfront and expect the relationship to absorb adjustments along the way. That works well in person. It does not work well remotely. The relationship-based flexibility that's natural in a Buenos Aires session room doesn't survive the timezone gap, and trying to maintain it remotely is what kills profitability on otherwise good projects.

The lesson wasn't that one approach is better. It was that I had to choose one approach per project, communicate it clearly upfront, and not switch midway. Most of the painful projects in my history were ones where the cultural framing flipped halfway through.

What I learned about infrastructure

The infrastructure that actually mattered, in order:

Predictable file delivery. Not "I'll email you the stems" but a structured delivery system the artist can rely on. Every project, same format, same notification, same download experience. Sounds boring. Eliminates an entire category of "did you get it?" follow-ups.

Asynchronous review with synchronous decisions. Async review is fine. Async decisions are not. The tools that let an artist leave timestamped comments on a mix are useful. The decisions about which comments to action have to happen in a 30-minute live conversation, or they don't happen.

A documented session workflow that doesn't require me. If everything in the session depends on me being awake, the practice doesn't scale across timezones. Session structure, naming conventions, and delivery process have to be legible to whoever's continuing the work, including future-me at 6am.

The honest summary

The remote freelance practice worked because the scope was tighter, the cultural framing was explicit, and the infrastructure was boring on purpose. None of that is a tools problem. All of it is a discipline problem.

The next time someone asks me whether a fully remote audio practice is "viable," I'll tell them yes, but only if they're willing to scope like a project manager and document like a software team. Most freelancers aren't, which is why most remote practices stay small.

Five years in, the discipline is still the hardest part.
payload.title: Five years of remote freelance work

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
The cluster of notes I've been writing on music provenance has stayed at the conceptual layer. The argument is sound, but readers who haven't built infrastructure tend to walk away with a fuzzy picture of how this would actually surface in the real world.

This is the concrete version. Five layers where provenance would show up, what each looks like in practice, and one mockup of the layer that matters most.

Layer 1: The DAW

The signature attaches at creation. That means the surface where it first lives is the DAW (the digital audio workstation, which is the recording and editing software an artist or engineer works in: Pro Tools, Logic, Ableton Live, FL Studio).

Imagine a small indicator next to a session or a track: "Signed by [author] · [date] · verified." Click it, see the credential, optionally inspect the chain back to the original signing. No new workflow for the artist. The signature happens in the background when the session is created or rendered, in the same way DAWs already auto-fill metadata about plugin chains and sample rates today.

The interesting design question isn't whether a DAW can do this. It's which DAW does it first, and whether they expose it as a feature or treat it as plumbing. My bet is plumbing for the first generation, then a visible "credential" indicator once the rights pipeline downstream actually consumes the data.

Layer 2: The distributor

Distributors take a finished master and turn it into a delivery package: audio files, metadata, artwork, identifiers, formatted for whatever each streaming platform expects.

The natural surface for provenance here is the delivery package itself. A new field, or a new structured block within the existing message, that carries the credential alongside the existing identifier set: ISRC (the unique code assigned to every recording), ISWC (the unique code assigned to every underlying composition), and the rest. If DDEX (the music industry's main standards body for digital supply chain messaging) standardizes the field, every distributor can populate it without inventing their own format. If DDEX doesn't, individual distributors will populate it differently, and we'll have a mess to clean up later.

This is the least visible layer to artists and the most consequential layer for whether the system works. The distributor is the choke point. If the credential survives the distributor's package, it survives everything downstream. If it doesn't, the rest is irrelevant.

Layer 3: The streaming platform backend

Spotify, Apple Music, Amazon Music, YouTube Music. Each ingests millions of tracks daily. Each runs internal systems for rights matching, royalty calculation, and payout routing.

The streaming platform doesn't need to show provenance to listeners. It needs to receive it from the distributor and use it for its own rights and royalty plumbing. This is quiet infrastructure. No UI, no badges, no consumer-facing change. Just cleaner matching, fewer reconciliation failures, less money flowing into the black box (the pool of collected royalties that can't be paid out because the system can't figure out who they belong to).

The reason this matters: most of the economic value of provenance is captured at this layer, even though nothing about it is visible to anyone who isn't a rights operations engineer at a streaming platform. The boring layer is where the dollars are.

Layer 4: The royalty statement

This is the layer that matters most to labels and the one I'd argue is the strongest case for adopting provenance in the first place.

A label's monthly royalty statement currently shows revenue by track, territory, platform, and rights type. With provenance, it shows something new: a column distinguishing verified line items from unverified ones. The unverified column shrinks every quarter as the back catalog gets re-credentialed and as new releases come through with provenance from day one. The verified column is money the label knows is theirs without manual reconciliation.

Royalty statement with provenance, illustrative mockup
A monthly royalty statement showing five tracks. Each row has a track title, territory, gross revenue, and a verification status. Three rows are verified and reconciled. Two rows are unverified and require manual matching. A summary at the bottom shows total verified revenue versus total unverified revenue.

ROYALTY STATEMENT · APRIL 2026 · ILLUSTRATIVE
