$2,157.10

VERIFIED 79% · UNVERIFIED 21% · ILLUSTRATIVE

The first time a label CFO sees this view, the abstract argument about provenance becomes concrete. The verified column is reconciled, paid, and closed. The unverified column is where the work still needs to happen. The split tells you exactly where to focus the metadata cleanup budget.

Layer 5: The optional listener layer

A future Spotify, Apple Music, or YouTube Music app could surface a small "verified human" indicator on a song's now-playing screen or detail page. Click it, see the credential, optionally see who else along the chain is verified.

The word optional matters. This isn't gatekeeping. Songs without provenance don't get filtered out or downranked. Listeners who don't care never have to see the indicator. Listeners who do care get a passive signal they can choose to act on.

Most listeners will never engage with this layer. That's fine. The system was never built for them. It was built for the rights and royalty pipeline upstream of where they listen, and the listener-facing surface is a nice-to-have, not the point.

What changes when these layers work together

The reason to walk through them all is that none of them work in isolation. A signed credential at the DAW with no distributor support is a museum piece. Distributor support without streaming platform ingestion is a metadata tax. Streaming platform ingestion without statement-level visibility for labels is invisible plumbing nobody asked for.

The system has to land on all five layers to function. That's why this isn't a one-vendor problem. It's an industry problem. The DAW makers, the distributors, the streaming platforms, and the standards bodies (DDEX foremost) all have to participate.

The technology to do this exists. The standards work to do this is achievable. The will to coordinate across the layers is the thing that's been missing, and it's the thing that'll change in the next two to three years whether anyone wants it to or not, because the math on the alternative (detection at scale) keeps getting worse.

That's the picture. Five layers, one system, no central gatekeeper, and a royalty statement that finally reads as cleanly as it should have all along.
payload.title: Five layers, one system

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
Detection-first approaches usually get argued on accuracy. Detectors produce false positives, generation outpaces classification, models evolve faster than the systems built to catch them. True, repeated often enough that the accuracy argument doesn't carry much new weight.

The argument that hasn't been made enough is the cost one. Accuracy is fixable on long timescales. Cost structure isn't.

What detection costs to operate

Every detector has to evaluate every piece of content. That's the job. At platform scale, "every piece of content" means more than 250 million existing tracks, plus more than 100,000 new uploads daily, plus the third of those that arrive AI-generated. Deezer reported 50,000 fully AI-generated uploads daily by late 2025. Spotify removed over 75 million spam tracks in 2025 alone. Each evaluation has a compute cost.

Each new generative model release has a retraining cost. Each new bypass technique has an integration cost. Detection's costs scale with both content volume and model sophistication. Both numbers go up every quarter.

This isn't unique to AI music. It's the same cost curve that's eaten antivirus, deepfake detection, plagiarism flagging, and every other classifier-versus-generator system that's tried to police a producer with infinite output potential. The classifier always loses on cost before it loses on accuracy.

What AI generation costs to produce

A model gets trained once. After that, generating one more track is a marginal-cost operation that approaches zero. Inference on consumer hardware is measurable in cents per track. On dedicated infrastructure, less.

Producers of AI music aren't running detectors against themselves. They aren't paying for moderation. Their cost curve goes down with scale, not up. Every model improvement and every efficiency gain on the generation side widens the gap detection has to close.

Generation costs trend toward zero. Detection costs trend with volume and complexity. The two curves diverge, and they diverge faster every quarter.

COST CURVES UNDER GROWING VOLUME

COST

VOLUME →

Detection

Generation

Provenance

Illustrative. Verification cost is independent of generation volume.

Why this hides in current accounting

The cost of detection at most labels and platforms is split across lines. Moderation budgets. Dispute resolution. Royalty leakage from false positives. Legal exposure when an artist's account gets flagged wrongly. Catalog contamination when AI tracks slip through the threshold and accumulate streams.

None of those lines is labeled "the cost of fighting an asymmetric arms race." But that's what they sum to. The line item doesn't exist because the architectural choice is invisible to the accounting layer.

What changes with provenance

Verification at creation is a one-time signing event. Every downstream check is a hash comparison. The compute cost of verifying a signature is close enough to zero that platform-scale verification doesn't show up as a budget line at all.

Verification cost is independent of generation volume. It's also independent of model sophistication. A signed track from 2026 verifies the same way in 2030 regardless of how much better the AI got in between. Detection's cost curve climbs. Provenance's cost curve sits flat.

That's the structural difference that matters. Not that provenance is more accurate. Not that provenance is more elegant. Provenance is the only architecture in this category whose cost curve doesn't punish operating at scale.

The companies that solve this don't solve it by buying better detection. They solve it by changing what they're paying for. Provenance flips the budget from ongoing cost of catching up to one-time cost of signing once.

That's the trade that ends the arms race. Not because detection got beaten. Because the side paying for it stopped paying.

Detection scales the wrong way. Provenance scales the way infrastructure does.
payload.title: Detection scales the wrong way

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
When you walk into an event, someone might hand you a name tag. It works, mostly. People can read it and the conversation moves. But name tags fail in obvious ways. They get printed wrong. They fall off. Someone else's gets handed to you by mistake. The system doesn't know who you are. It only knows what the tag says.

Fingerprints are different. Nobody assigns them. Two people can't share them. They can't fall off, can't be transferred, can't be confused with someone else's. The system doesn't have to write anything down. It just has to look.

Music has been running on a name tag system for fifty years.

Name tag
A handwritten name tag with a torn corner and a scribbled-out name, representing how administrative identifiers fail when mistyped, lost, or transferred.

HELLO MY NAME IS

Name tagAssigned, mistyped, lost

Fingerprint
A fingerprint pattern of concentric loops, representing a cryptographic identity that is intrinsic to the work, mathematically unique, and cannot be transferred to a different file.

FingerprintBorn with the file, cannot be transferred

How the name tag system fails

Every recording released through the modern industry gets identifying codes. There's a code for the recording, a different code for the underlying composition, another for the person who made it, another for the album. These codes get issued by administrative bodies, typed into databases, and propagated through distribution.

When the codes work, the system functions. When they don't, and they often don't, the music wears the wrong name tag. The code got mistyped at the distributor. The streaming service has a different code than the rights holder. Two recordings somehow share a code. The code was missing entirely.

The result is hundreds of millions of dollars in royalties that can't find their way home. The recording exists. The artist exists. The streams happened. But the chain that connects "this stream" to "this artist's bank account" runs through a series of name tags, and any one being wrong is enough to break it.

The pile keeps growing because the volume keeps growing. Around a hundred thousand new tracks get uploaded daily. Each gets name-tagged. A meaningful percentage will be wrong, missing, or inconsistent across the systems that need to agree.

What a fingerprint system would look like

A music file doesn't have natural fingerprints the way a human does. But it can have something equivalent: a cryptographic signature derived from the audio itself, generated at the moment the file is created, mathematically guaranteed to be unique to that recording, impossible to transfer to a different file, verifiable by anyone with access to the file.

This isn't speculative. The cryptographic primitives are several decades old. They secure financial transactions, software updates, government identity systems. They're well understood and routine in adjacent industries. What they haven't done yet is replace the name tag system in music.

If music adopted them, the file's identity would be inseparable from the file. The administrative codes wouldn't go away. They'd stick around as labels, useful for human readers and legacy systems. But the underlying identity wouldn't depend on them anymore. When the codes disagree, the fingerprint settles it.

Why this hasn't happened yet

The technology is decades old. The reason adoption hasn't happened is coordination, not capability.

Recording software, distributors, streaming platforms, and collection societies all have to make adjustments that don't benefit any one of them individually until enough of the others have moved. The first to ship loses the marketing battle to whoever waits. The last to ship gets the benefit without the cost. That's the worst possible incentive structure for the system that makes the change first.

But the math says somebody eventually does. The name tag pile keeps growing. The cost of running the current system keeps rising. At some point the first major player ships the fingerprint version, and everyone else follows because they're the only ones still bleeding.

Until then, the artists with the wrong name tag pay the cost. The system pretends the cost is unavoidable.

It isn't.
payload.title: Fingerprints, not name tags

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
"Provenance" has become a crowded word in music infrastructure. Detection vendors use it. Stem-matching products use it. Watermarking systems use it. Each product solves a real problem, but they're not the same problem, and treating them as one provenance category obscures what the work actually requires.

There's a clean line that separates real provenance from the products borrowing the language. Falsifiability.

Cryptographic signatures cannot be falsified in the technical sense. Detection scores can be. Stem-matching results can be. The distinction sounds academic until you put it in front of a royalty dispute, at which point it becomes the entire point.

What unfalsifiable means

Cryptographic signatures are unfalsifiable in a specific technical sense. Either the signature verifies against the public key, or it doesn't. There's no probability score. No confidence interval. No edge case where the math is "almost right." A valid signature is proof that the holder of the private key signed the data. Period.

This is the same primitive that authorizes wire transfers at every bank. The same one that signs every code update an operating system installs. The same one that secures every TLS connection a browser makes. The same one on the chip in a passport. The math has been audited continuously for decades and the failure modes are well understood. When signatures fail, it's because private keys leak, not because the system is uncertain.

Unfalsifiable doesn't mean unhackable. It means that within the boundaries of the system, the claim is binary. If the signature verifies, the signer signed it. If it doesn't, they didn't. There is no third state.

The pushback worth answering

The pushback at this point is that signatures verify keys, not people. True, and beside the point. Identity binding has established solutions across passports, banking, and code-signing certificates. Content-based detection has no solution at the math layer. Provenance moves the dispute from where it cannot be settled to where it can. The remaining work is operational, not epistemic.

What falsifiable means in detection

Detection systems and pattern-matching systems produce falsifiable claims by design. A detector says "this track is 87% likely to be AI-generated." A stem matcher says "this matches a verified human stem with 94% confidence." Both claims include their own uncertainty.

Falsifiable claims are useful for triage. They are not useful for adjudication. In a royalty dispute, "94% confident" is an opening argument, not an answer. The other side argues for the 6%. The case goes to human review, then to legal review, then settles or doesn't. The dispute extends.

This is true even for very accurate detection systems. A detector with 99.9% accuracy is wrong on one of every thousand tracks it evaluates. At any meaningful platform scale, that produces enough false positives to feed continuous disputes. Better models lower the rate. They don't change the category of claim. Probabilistic claims remain probabilistic regardless of how good the probability gets.

Detection versus provenance: probabilistic against binary
Detection produces probabilistic, falsifiable claims that fall somewhere on a continuous zero to one hundred percent scale, with an example reading at eighty-seven percent. Provenance produces binary, unfalsifiable claims with only two possible states, valid or invalid.

DETECTION PRODUCES

0%
100%

87%

Probabilistic. Falsifiable.

PROVENANCE PRODUCES

VALID
INVALID

Binary. Unfalsifiable.

Why music needs the unfalsifiable primitive

Music distribution runs on disputes. Royalty splits between writers, performers, producers, masterers. Mechanical licenses, performance rights, sync rights. Master recording versus composition. Sample clearances. Public domain edge cases. Black box payments where the rights holder cannot be identified. When The MLC opened in 2021, twenty-one DSPs transferred $424 million in historical unmatched royalties that could not be paid because rights holders could not be identified. The metadata layer is structurally broken.

A verification system that ends disputes is worth a different category of money than one that contributes evidence to disputes. Banks pay for unfalsifiable signatures because they end the dispute about whether a transfer was authorized. Operating systems pay for code signing because it ends the dispute about whether an update came from the vendor. Music has the same dispute volume and currently has neither.

Detection products will improve. Accuracy will get better. False positive rates will get lower. None of that changes the fact that the claim is falsifiable, which means it can be challenged. Music does not need claims that can be challenged less often. It needs proofs that cannot be challenged at all.

The line between provenance and everything-else-that-uses-the-word-provenance runs along this distinction. If the claim is statistical, it's evidence. If the claim is mathematical, it's proof. Evidence is useful. Proof is the layer the industry actually needs.

Falsifiability is the line. Triage tools sit on one side. Infrastructure sits on the other.
payload.title: Falsifiability is the line

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
A track today rarely originates from a single act of authorship in a single DAW. It assembles. A producer pulls a drum loop from a sample marketplace, generates a stem from a music gen AI tool, drops in a vocal sent over by a collaborator, layers in a synth line they played themselves, and renders the master out the back of the DAW.

The DAW signs the assembly. That part is necessary. It is not sufficient.

If the upstream sources that contributed to the file are not signed at their own layer, the DAW's signature is binding only on the act of putting things together. It says nothing about what the things are or where they came from. That is the part of the chain where the most consequential questions live.

Three upstream surfaces

Modern music has at least three categories of upstream creation that need to sign at their own layer.

Sample and loop marketplaces are the first. A producer downloads a kick pattern, a melodic loop, a vocal hook. Each of those was created by someone at some point. The natural moment for that contribution to be signed is at upload to the marketplace. The marketplace already verifies producers at signup, manages payouts, and holds licensing terms. Signing the file at upload is small additional work on top of relationships that already exist.

Music gen AI tools are the second. Suno, Udio, ElevenLabs Music, and whatever ships next. The natural moment for those outputs to be signed is at generation, with the model identifier and the input prompt or seed bound into the manifest. This is operationally trivial. The tool already knows what model produced the output and what prompt drove it. The signature binds that information to the file in a way that survives downstream.

Collaborator stems are the third. Vocal takes, instrument stems, edits sent across by a co-writer or a session player. The natural moment to sign is at handoff. Every modern handoff path (Splice, Dropbox, the embedded sharing built into recent DAWs) could make this trivial. Today the file leaves the collaborator's hands carrying nothing structural about who made it.

The DAW pulls these signed inputs in. Its signature at export references them in the manifest. The output is not "signed by the DAW" alone. It is "signed by the DAW, built from these signed components, here are their credentials." A reader downstream can verify the chain end to end, or pick any link to inspect.

What breaks if any layer is missing

UPSTREAM CHAIN

SURFACE
SIGNS AT
STATUS

Sample marketplace
upload
✓ signed

Music gen AI
generation
✓ signed

Collaborator stem
handoff
✓ signed

Unsigned source
—
✗ chain broken

DAW
export
✓ signed

The chain is only as complete as its weakest link.

The chain is only as complete as its weakest link.

The temptation is to assume that signing the DAW output is enough. It is not.

A sample taken from an unsigned marketplace and assembled into a track produces a DAW signature that says "I signed this assembly." Downstream, a rights query asking who created the underlying loop has no answer. The signature does not deny it. It just does not speak to it. The chain continues with a gap, and a gap is what disputes drive through.

A gen-AI stem dropped in without its model and input manifest signed at the source produces the same problem. The DAW knows the stem was placed in the session. It does not know what the stem is. A label downstream looking at AI exposure across a release portfolio cannot answer the question "what percentage of this release used generative material" by reading the signed manifest. They can only read the assembly.

Multiply this across a hundred thousand new tracks released daily and the unsigned-input problem is the same kind of metadata mess the name tag system produces today, just relocated upstream.

Different verbs, same primitive

Each surface signs differently because each surface does different work.

The DAW signs at export because that is when the assembly is finalized.

The marketplace signs at upload because that is when the contribution enters the supply.

The gen AI tool signs at generation because that is when the output exists.

The collaborator signs at handoff because that is when the file leaves their custody.

Different verbs, different moments, same cryptographic primitive. The system does not care which surface a signature came from. It only cares that every link in the chain checks out.

The chain is the asset

The system's value is not in any single signature. It is in the chain.

A signed assembly with unsigned inputs is half the work, and the half that is missing is the half that is hardest to add later. Once the file is in distribution, identifying which loop came from where, which stem was generated, and which take was sent by whom is forensic work. With signatures at the source, it is a query.

The cryptographic primitives can do this. They have been doing it in other industries for decades. The question is not whether the upstream surfaces have the technology. It is whether they ship before the assembly layer demands it, and whether they coordinate on a shared format the DAW can consume.

The DAW's signature is the easy part. The inputs are where the work is.
payload.title: Signing the inputs at the source

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
Any conversation about a new identification system for music eventually arrives at the same question: what about the music that already exists?

There are around 250 million tracks on streaming services. Almost none of them carry creation-time provenance, because the cryptographic frameworks to do so didn't exist when most of them were recorded. If the industry adopts a new identifier substrate going forward, what happens to everything that came before?

The short answer: nothing has to change. The longer answer is more useful.

Backward compatibility by design

Existing identifiers don't get retired. The codes that already identify a recording, a composition, an artist, an album: those keep working exactly the way they work now. The new substrate identifier is an additional layer underneath, not a replacement on top.

A recording with an existing code keeps using that code. Streaming services keep reading it. Collection societies keep matching against it. The new identifier sits beneath as an additional reference point. When the existing codes disagree across systems (which is the entire reason the substrate exists), the underlying identifier resolves the conflict.

This is the same pattern that domain names follow. The actual address of a server is a numerical IP address. Humans don't memorize those. We use domain names because they're easier to type and remember. Both layers exist simultaneously. The domain name resolves to the IP address, but the IP address is the source of truth. If somebody mistypes the domain name, the IP address is still correct underneath.

Substrate beneath legacy codes
A diagram showing four existing music identifier codes as visible labels on top, all resolving down to a single cryptographic substrate identifier underneath. Both layers coexist; the substrate is the source of truth when the visible codes disagree.
Existing identifiers (visible, used by every system)

recording code
composition code
artist code
album code

cryptographic substrate identifier
Source of truth. Resolves conflicts.

The legacy attestation question

For a new release, the recording software generates a creation-time identifier and signs it. There's a producer, there's a session, there's a moment of creation, there's a key. That whole flow is missing for music recorded ten or fifty years ago. There's no session to sign at. There may not even be a living producer.

What works for legacy catalog is a lighter-weight credential. A rights holder attests that they own a specific recording, that the existing identifiers point to it, and that the corresponding ownership splits are accurate. The attestation itself is signed. It doesn't prove who originally made the recording, since that information was never captured in a verifiable way. It does establish a verifiable claim of ownership going forward.

This is meaningfully better than the current state, where ownership assertions exist as unsigned database entries that get overwritten every time something changes. Legacy attestation locks the ownership state at a moment in time, signed by the party making the claim, and provides an audit trail when ownership later transfers or gets disputed.

Why this matters

The hardest part of any infrastructure transition is what happens to the existing infrastructure. Replace-everything-at-once never works. Catalogs are too large, contracts are too entangled, technical systems are too interdependent. Real transitions happen in layers, with old systems remaining functional while new systems get built underneath them.

The substrate model is built for that kind of transition. Future works get full creation-time provenance. Past works get whatever level of attestation is feasible to retrofit. Both work. Neither requires the catalogs of every label, distributor, and collection society to be renumbered.

Over time, as more new music ships with full provenance and more legacy works get attested, the credentialed portion of the global catalog grows. The unmatched portion shrinks. Nothing has to be retired for that to happen, which is a good thing, because nothing was going to be retired anyway.
payload.title: What happens to old music?

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
Most discussions of music provenance assume the artist signing a recording is somebody the system already knows about. They're on a label. They're registered with a performance rights organization. They have a distributor account. The institutions that already verify their identity provide the trust anchor that makes the cryptographic signature meaningful.

That covers a meaningful slice of working musicians. It doesn't cover the long tail.

The institutional shortcut

A signature only proves what the system already trusts. If a recording is signed by Maria Sanchez, and the verification system knows that Maria Sanchez has been registered with a rights organization for six years, has released three albums through Distribution Company X, and has a verified Recording Academy membership, the signature has real weight. It's not just a string of numbers. It's a string of numbers attached to an identity that several independent institutions have already vetted.

When the institutions are already in place, the cryptographic layer is the easy part.

What happens when the institutions aren't there

Consider the producer who's been releasing tracks under a pseudonym from a city without a music industry presence. They have no rights organization membership. They have no label. They self-distribute through a service that doesn't perform identity verification. They're a real human making real music. They have nothing the verification system can hang an identity claim on.

Under the current administrative system, this isn't a problem because the current system doesn't really verify identity either. Anyone with an email address can upload a track. The verification gap exists, but it's hidden because the system isn't trying to do verification in the first place.

A provenance framework changes that. If the framework promises verifiable authorship, it has to deliver verifiable authorship for everyone, not just artists who happen to be embedded in the institutions. Otherwise the framework re-creates the same gatekeeping it claimed to dissolve.

What a solution looks like, and what it requires

Who vouches for the independent artist?

Most discussions of music provenance assume the artist signing a recording is somebody the system already knows. There's a label, a performance rights organization that registered them years ago, a distributor account under their legal name. Those institutions already verify identity, and that verification is the trust anchor that makes a cryptographic signature mean anything.

That covers a meaningful slice of working musicians. It doesn't cover the long tail.

The institutional shortcut

A signature only proves what the system already trusts. Say a recording is signed by Maria Sanchez. The verification system knows that name: six years with a rights organization, three albums through a real distributor, a Recording Academy membership on file. The signature carries weight. It isn't just a string of numbers. It's a string of numbers attached to an identity several independent institutions have already vetted.

When the institutions are in place, the cryptographic layer is the easy part.

What happens when the institutions aren't there

Consider the producer releasing tracks under a pseudonym from a city with no music industry to speak of. No label, no rights organization, a self-distribution service that never asks who they are. A real person making real music, and nothing for the verification system to hang an identity claim on.

Right now this isn't a problem, because the current system doesn't really verify identity either. Anyone with an email address can upload a track. The gap is there. It's just hidden, because nobody's claiming to close it.

A provenance framework changes that. If it promises verifiable authorship, it has to deliver verifiable authorship for everyone, not only the artists already embedded in the institutions. Otherwise it recreates the exact gatekeeping it claimed to dissolve.

What a solution looks like, and what it requires

Tiered approaches work in theory. The foundation is a stable signing key the producer keeps across releases, the same identity over time even when no central authority has formally verified who's behind it. From there it climbs.

Three tiers of identity verification
A stacked diagram showing three tiers of identity verification for independent artists. The wide base tier is a stable signing key over time. The narrower middle tier adds professional association membership. The narrowest top tier adds government identity. Each tier builds on the one below.

Base tier
Stable signing key over time
Reputation accumulates around the key

Middle tier
Adds professional association
membership

Top tier
Adds government identity
(optional)

The base tier is the one that matters for the long tail, because it asks nothing of an institution that doesn't exist yet. A producer who has signed forty releases over five years has built something real. No central authority vouches for the legal name, and the work still adds up to a track record anyone can verify against a single key.

That's the theory. In practice, the base tier needs coordination among professional associations, distributors, and the developers shipping the recording software that issues the keys to begin with. None of that is built. Until it is, the framework serves the artists who were already easy to verify and comes up short for the rest, and the rest are the same people the current system serves worst.

This is the part I don't have a finished answer for. The cryptographic primitives are decades old. The infrastructure they lean on, for artists without institutional ties, hasn't been built. That's the work the next decade has to do, and the people who do it well will determine whether provenance becomes a real democratization of authorship credit or another structure that rewards the artists who were already winning.
payload.title: Who vouches for the independent artist?

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
I've written project proposals in this industry using six acronyms in two sentences. I knew the reader already knew the acronyms. The writing wasn't for them. It was for the version of me that needed to feel credentialed in front of someone whose approval I wanted.

That instinct is the thing this Note is about.

There's a pattern in how the music industry writes about itself, and once you see it, it's hard to unsee. Trade press written for other trade press. Conference panels written for the people on the next panel. White papers written for the consultants who'll cite them. Podcast episodes recorded for the small group of people who already know the acronyms.

It's a closed loop. The audience the writers have in mind isn't the listener. It isn't the working artist. It isn't the curious journalist outside the music vertical. It's the same fifty people in the same rooms talking to each other in a shared dialect.

This isn't a complaint about insider language. Every industry has its own jargon, and shorthand is useful when you're moving fast among people who share context. The problem is what happens when the shorthand becomes the only register the industry can write in.

What the incentives actually reward

I want to be careful here, because the easy version of this critique points fingers at individuals. That's not where the problem lives. The problem lives in the incentive structure that the writing happens inside.

Writers who use insider dialect get invited to panels. Writers who don't, don't. Writers who name-drop the right acronyms get cited in the right trade publications. Writers who stop and explain the acronym for the broader reader get treated as less serious by their peers, even when their writing is more useful to anyone outside the room.

The result isn't that any individual writer is acting in bad faith. The result is a field where the rational career move is to write inaccessibly, and where the people who write accessibly pay a small career cost for the choice. Multiplied across thousands of writers and decades of accumulation, the field gets the writing it incentivizes.

That's the structural piece. It's not about villains. It's about a system that rewards a behavior nobody would defend if you asked them about it directly.

Who's already doing it right

Some of the best writers in this space already write accessibly, and it's worth naming them as a pattern even when I'm not naming them by name. The trade journalists who explain rather than credential. The label and distribution executives who publish public memos in plain English. The music lawyers who break down DMCA cases and copyright rulings for readers who aren't lawyers. The royalty operations specialists who write threads explaining why a statement looks the way it looks.

They exist. They're not rare. They're just systematically less visible than the writers who cater to the closed loop, because the loop's amplification mechanisms (panels, podcasts, trade press features) reward the closed register more than the open one.

If you're one of those writers, this Note isn't about you. It's about what I'm trying to learn from you.

What gets lost when the loop wins

When the only audience is insiders, writing stops being clarifying and starts being credentialing. The point of saying "DDEX," "MLC distribution," or "ERN deliveries" stops being to communicate and starts being to signal: I belong in this room. I know the codes.

That's a perfectly human instinct. I've fallen for it more than once. It's also the instinct that quietly excludes everyone the industry exists to serve.

Listeners pay for music. Indirectly through subscriptions, directly through tickets and merchandise, attentionally through every minute they choose to spend with a song instead of something else. They fund the entire system. And almost nothing the industry writes about how the system works is written with them in mind.

Working artists fall in the same gap. Most of them don't know how their own royalty pipeline works, not because they're incurious, but because the writing that explains it is impenetrable. When the writing is impenetrable, the system stays opaque. When the system stays opaque, the people who already understand it stay in charge by default.

The standard I'm trying to hold myself to

Everything I publish on this site uses the same rule now: industry readers can read it, listeners can read it, and I don't care if it reads as overkill for the experts. Acronyms get spelled out. Concepts get defined when needed. The argument doesn't depend on the reader having insider context.

This isn't a marketing tactic. It's a position about who the writing is for.

I don't always get it right. There are pieces I've written where I caught myself reaching for the credentialing word and let it through anyway because it felt natural. The discipline is ongoing. The point is to keep choosing the plain word more often than the coded one, and to forgive yourself when you slip and try again on the next piece.

The invitation

If you're already writing accessibly inside the industry, you're not who this Note is about. You're who I'm trying to learn from.

If you're not, the cost of changing is smaller than it looks. The first time you spell out an acronym in a piece your peers expected you to leave coded, you'll feel like you gave something up. You didn't. You just wrote for a bigger audience than the one you were used to. That audience includes the listener who pays for the system, the artist who's living inside it, and the journalist outside the music vertical who's trying to understand it. All three deserve writing they can follow. The industry didn't lose anything by giving it to them.

The closed loop doesn't end because somebody calls it out from outside. It ends because enough people inside it decide to write for the bigger room. I'm trying to. I hope more of us do.
payload.title: The music industry talks to itself in code

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
These are notes, not articles. Short pieces, each taking one idea as far as it goes, working out a single argument in public and in sequence. If you landed on one of them cold, this page is the map.

What a note is

A note is a three to four minute read that holds one idea and stops. It is not news commentary, and it is not written for search engines. Each one stakes a position and shows its work.

Once a note is published and dated, it does not get edited. There is one canonical version, and it stays the version it was the day it went up. If the thinking moves, that becomes a new note, not a quiet revision of an old one. You can trust that what you are reading is what was argued, when it was argued.

This page is the one exception. It describes the system, so it stays current.

Why they exist

The notes are building one case. Music is heading into an era where the question of who made what, and with which inputs, decides who gets paid. The current answer is detection, which guesses after the fact and can always be challenged. The argument here is that the answer has to be provenance instead. Identity that is cryptographic, bound to the work, and signed at the moment of creation, so it survives the life of the file.

The formal versions of this argument live as papers. The notes are where it gets worked out in the open, one piece at a time, where anyone can follow the reasoning and push on it.

How they are organized

The notes sit in one index, newest first, each tagged by topic. Tags are the map. Follow one and you get every note that touches that thread. Some notes build on earlier ones and link back when they do, but there is no required order. Each one stands on its own.

Above the notes sit the pillar essays. Those are the long-form anchors, each backed by a paper. A note assumes you have either read the pillars or are willing to.

Where to start

Start with the pillars. Provenance Over Detection carries the core argument. Provenance as Substrate carries the technical case underneath it. Every note hangs off one or the other. From there, jump to whichever question brought you here, or work through the index and watch the argument accumulate.

How to follow

There is a feed, and the feed is the spine of all of it. Subscribe via RSS and new notes arrive as they publish. If you would rather read by email, point a service like Blogtrottr or Feedrabbit at the feed and each note lands in your inbox, on your account, not a list of mine. There is no signup form here and no list I keep, by design. The work is here for anyone who wants it, on the reader's terms.

Nothing here is finished. That is the reason for working it out where you can watch it happen.
payload.title: Start here

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
When the business of music changes shape, somebody sets the new terms. It is never a vote. It is whoever walked in holding leverage from the last arrangement.

Leverage is portable. It survives the jump from one format to the next, one technology to the next, because it lives in capital, in catalog, and in the ability to make a lawsuit expensive. The thing that does not survive the jump is the ability to make the work.

Origination is not leverage. It never has been.

The same tier, every cycle

The same tier loses the same way each time. Not the loudest one. The one whose price is set for it.

Streaming is the clean case. The companies that controlled the recordings negotiated their rates and took their equity. The songwriters negotiated nothing. Their mechanical rate is set by the Copyright Royalty Board under a compulsory license, which is a polite way of saying their price is decided in a room they are not in. Fifteen years on, the split between the tiers is exactly where it started. Performers got no equity. The self-releasing artist got a per-stream rate measured in fractions of a cent.

Now watch the AI settlements. Most of the majors cut deals. The one holdout is bargaining for more, not shut out of the room. The independent licensing bloc cut a deal. The publishers are doing both at once, licensing the willing and litigating the rest, because they remember what administered means and they are trying to convert it to negotiated before the terms set. The individual creators are in class actions, which is what you file when there is no table to sit at.

Same three tiers. Same order. Same outcome forming in real time.

What Merlin proves

The reflex, every time, is to protest. Sign the letter. Name the villain. The reflex is rational, because protest is the only instrument available to people who cannot show up as a bloc. But a grievance is not a seat. Being right about fairness has never moved a rate.

There is one counterexample, and it proves the rule rather than breaking it. The independent labels got a seat through Merlin, by doing the unglamorous thing: aggregating into a single licensable unit over almost twenty years. When the AI platforms came negotiating, Merlin walked in holding a bloc, and got the deal the atomized could not.

The seat was not handed over for being independent, or for being right. It was manufactured out of aggregation. Which means it is buildable. It just has to be built before the terms harden, not after.

The floor below the bloc

Aggregation requires something to aggregate. A bloc of labels works because each label is already a fixed, identifiable unit of rights. The individual creator is not.

Their work carries name-tag metadata that breaks at the first transformation point and is gone by the second. You cannot aggregate what you cannot identify. And you cannot identify a catalog at scale with a system that loses the author every time a file is re-encoded, re-uploaded, or remixed. I have spent a dozen notes on why that identifier system fails and what replaces it. The short version: identity has to be cryptographic and bound to the work, not written in a field the next tool overwrites. New work gets signed at creation, at each layer. Back catalog gets a signed attestation of ownership instead. Both produce the same thing: an identity that survives the life of the work.

That identity is the instrument. It gives an atomized creator something portable and verifiable to bring to a bloc, without first signing to a company already inside one. It makes the bottom tier aggregable, and aggregable is the only precondition for a seat that has ever held.

With one honest catch. The creator furthest outside the institutions, no label, no rights organization, is the one with nothing to anchor that identity to, and that trust layer is the part nobody has built yet. The instrument is real. The hardest version of it is still unfinished, for exactly the people who need it most.

This is not a better filing system. The metadata problem and the leverage problem are the same problem wearing two faces. Royalties leak and the originator tier never sits at the table for one reason: there is no verifiable identity that survives the life of the work.

The seat is manufactured

So stop calling provenance an upgrade to the identifier system. It is that. But that framing buries the stakes.

Provenance is the first leverage instrument the people who actually make the music have ever been in a position to build for themselves.

The seat was never given to anyone. It got manufactured, every time, by whoever built the instrument first. The only question is whether the tier that makes the work builds one before the next set of terms hardens, or files another class action after.
payload.title: The seat was never given

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
Provenance has the same problem TLS solved in the 1990s. Multiple vendors building proprietary verification stacks doesn't add up to a verification chain. It adds up to a coordination problem nobody owns.

The pitch from any provenance vendor sounds reasonable in isolation. We sign tracks at upload. We verify at distribution. We integrate with the partners we have. The chain works inside our system.

The chain working inside one system isn't the goal. The goal is a chain that works across the industry, because music doesn't move through one system. It moves through dozens.

What "interoperable" actually requires

A label uploads a track to its distributor. The distributor delivers to seven DSPs. Three DSPs run their own AI-detection systems. Two pass through to publishers. The publisher routes mechanical royalties to a collection society in territory and a different one in territory B. A sync agency clears the master separately. A sample of the track gets used in a derivative work that gets uploaded to a different distributor.

That's a single track. Every party in that chain is a verification surface. If the verification primitive at each surface is proprietary to a different vendor, the chain has to reconcile across vendors at every handoff. The dispute layer doesn't end. It just moves to a different table.

Open primitives don't have this problem. The verification math is the same regardless of which vendor's tooling did the signing or the verifying. Anyone with the public key and the protocol can verify any signature. That's the structural property that lets a chain work across thousands of independent participants.

Why proprietary verification can't reach industry scale

A proprietary verification primitive is bilateral. Vendor A and any party that adopts vendor A's stack can verify each other. Anyone outside that stack has to either trust the vendor's word, run a different verification process, or treat the chain as broken at that handoff.

Bilateral verification doesn't end disputes. It moves them from "is this track signed" to "do we trust this vendor's signing." That's the same dispute, with new participants, in a different room.

Music distribution has tried this pattern repeatedly. Proprietary watermarking standards. Proprietary fingerprint catalogs. Proprietary rights management databases. Each one solved a problem inside its walls. None of them coordinated across the industry, because coordination requires a shared primitive, not a shared vendor.

A CHAIN IS ONLY AS OPEN AS ITS LINKS

PROPRIETARY

LBL

DST

✕

DSP

PUB

✕

SOC

OPEN

LBL

DST

DSP

PUB

SOC

A chain only works if every link uses the same primitive.

The historical precedent

TLS replaced the bilateral SSL implementations of the 1990s because email, banking, and commerce needed encryption that worked across vendors. PGP made signed messages possible because nobody could ship encrypted mail through any single vendor's proprietary system. IPFS specified content addressing as a public protocol because distributed storage needed every participant to verify the same way. X.509 certificates standardized identity binding because authentication had to scale beyond any single CA.

In each case, the open primitive came first, then the vendors built on top of it. The vendors that resisted standardization either lost or were forced into compatibility. The pattern isn't accidental. It's what scale requires.

Where the line actually runs

The argument isn't that nothing in provenance can be proprietary. Vendors can build closed implementations on top of an open primitive. They can offer closed user interfaces, closed key management products, closed monitoring dashboards. None of that breaks the chain.

What breaks the chain is closing the verification primitive itself. If the way a signature is structured, the way a public key is shared, the way a verification fails or succeeds de
payload.title: Open standards or no standards

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
The reporting called it a loss. On June 29 the Court of Appeal upheld the strike-out of Dave Rowntree's proposed class action against PRS, and roughly £200m in unmatched "black box" royalties stays where it was. Rowntree, a songwriter member who brought the case on behalf of PRS's writer members, accepted the outcome. Read as a headline, it is a defeat.

Read as a diagnosis, it is the most useful thing a court has said about music money in years. Lord Justice Miles did not rule that the distribution was fair in the abstract. He ruled that no fairer distribution could even be proposed, because the information required to build one does not exist. That reasoning locates the exact layer where the system breaks, and the layer sits upstream of everything the case was arguing about.

What the court actually found

The line worth keeping comes from the judgment ([2026] EWCA Civ 814). On why no better distribution method had been offered, Miles wrote that "the reason why no counterfactual has been advanced, even in general terms, is manifest: it is that the very data failure problem which has given rise to the black box royalties means that there is no plausible basis for suggesting a more accurate (let alone fairer) distribution."

That is not a defence of the black box. It is a description of how it forms. Money enters the system attached to a performance. The data that would route it to the right writer is incomplete, so it cannot be matched, so it pools. Every question that follows, who deserves it and by what formula, is asking how to allocate a loss that already happened. The court declined to referee that allocation, and it was right to. The injury occurred before distribution was ever in play. The failure is informational, and it is early.

Why the downstream fixes inherit the failure

Rowntree reached for litigation. The more common instinct is technical: better matching engines, cleaner registration, harder claiming against unidentified pools. Each of those operates on money that is already orphaned, which makes each one a recovery bet on information lost at the source.

This is the same shape as the argument in Provenance Over Detection. Detecting synthetic media after it exists is a probabilistic race that gets harder as the models improve, and it never reaches certainty because it is reconstructing something that was never captured. Royalty matching is that race in accounting clothes. It improves at the margins and the gap persists, because the gap is upstream of where matching works. A Court of Appeal has now said as much in its own domain: where the data failed at the point it should have been recorded, no method downstream can recover the truth, only estimate around it. That is the ceiling on every after-the-fact fix, stated from the bench.

What capture at the source changes

Provenance changes the tense of the problem. If contributor identity and splits are recorded and bound to the work at the moment of creation, the black box does not shrink for those works. It fails to form. The £200m is not recovered under this model, because under this model that money was never orphaned. The technical case for how that binding survives the life of a file is the subject of Provenance as Substrate; the point here is narrower, and the court supplied it.

Miles named the data failure as the reason no fairer distribution can be built. He was describing a dead end for litigation. The same fact read forward is a specification: fix the information where it originates, and the distribution problem never arrives.
payload.title: The court found the floor

payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
The most common pushback against cryptographic provenance for music is straightforward: what if the producer types the artist name wrong, gets the splits wrong, or signs the file before they realize the song title is misspelled?

The signature locks in whatever was signed. It doesn't validate that the data being signed was correct. This is true. It's also the reason cryptographic systems include correction mechanisms from the start.

The signature is permanent. The state isn't.

Think of it like git. You can't actually delete a commit. Once it's there, the hash is the hash, and the history is preserved. But you can add a new commit that fixes the error. The original wrong record stays in the audit trail. The corrected value becomes the current state. Anyone querying the system sees the correction. Anyone auditing the system can trace back to the original.

Provenance records work the same way. The original signed manifest exists forever. A signed correction record supersedes it. The current canonical state is whatever the most recent valid signed record says.

This is a feature. The current system, where metadata gets silently overwritten in databases when somebody notices an error, has no audit trail at all. With cryptographic correction, every change is signed, dated, and attributable to whoever made it.

Append-only correction chain
Three signed records arranged in a chain. The first contains an error and stays in the audit trail. The second is a correction. The third is the current canonical state, highlighted. Each record is signed and dated; none is overwritten.

Original

Correction

Current state

Each record signed. None overwritten. History preserved.

What different errors look like under this model

A typo in the artist's own field is a single-party correction. The original signer publishes a new signed record with the right value. No external coordination required. The corrected record becomes canonical.

A wrong split percentage is a multi-party correction. If the original record claimed Maria wrote 50% and Tomás wrote 50%, but Maria actually wrote 60%, both have to sign the correction. False attribution can't be silently fixed by one party rewriting the other party's contribution.

An identity-layer change (the artist's display name updates, their professional affiliation switches, their signing key gets compromised) propagates through the registry that holds their identity record. The producer doesn't have to re-sign every old release. The new identity information is read by anyone querying.

A disputed correction happens when the parties don't agree. One says X, the other says Y. The system doesn't auto-resolve. It records the dispute, flags the conflict, and waits for resolution outside the cryptographic layer: arbitration, court order, registry intervention. The conflict is preserved as part of the record, not hidden behind whoever updated last.

Why this matters

The current state of music metadata is that errors propagate silently and accumulate. A wrong code on a track at the distributor becomes a wrong code at the streaming service becomes a wrong code at the collection society. Nobody knows when it was introduced or by whom. Correcting it requires somebody to notice, and even then the correction is just an overwrite.

Under a cryptographic system, errors are visible and disputable. They have a date, an author, a chain of changes. A producer who signed wrong data has an audit trail showing what they claimed and when they corrected it. That's better than the alternative even before the matching-and-payment improvements show up.

The point isn't that the math eliminates human error. People will still type things wrong. The point is that the system makes the errors auditable instead of invisible, and gives every party a way to correct or dispute without losing the history.
payload.title: How a music file gets corrected
